explanation: After discarding 1z, the 1123s region serves as both a pair and a penchan: waits on 1s or 4s. Total ukeire 6. Any alternative discard breaks a working block.
Three complete sequences, a White Dragon pair, a 34s ryanmen, and a lone Red Dragon. The Red Dragon is the only tile not in a block. Discard it for tenpai on 2s / 5s.
Discarding 5s leaves a 23s ryanmen accepting 1s / 4s (ukeire 6). Discarding 2s instead leaves a 35s kanchan accepting only 4s (ukeire 3). Both are tenpai; 5s's wait is twice as wide.

Discarding 8p leaves a narrow kanchan 57p wait (2 tiles). Discarding 9p preserves the 58p kanchan on 6p and keeps improvement paths open — ukeire 3 vs 2. A deeper version of this lesson: measure waits by count, not by shanten alone.
Discard 9p → wait on 7p via the 89p penchan (ukeire 4). Discard 8p → shanpon wait on 9p / East (ukeire 4). Both are valid tenpai discards with identical acceptance.
Three sequences, two sou pairs (11s, 22s), and a lone White Dragon. Discarding the dragon produces a shanpon wait on 1s / 2s.
The 22334m region is the decision point. Discard 2m → 2334m ryanmen on 1m / 4m (ukeire 7). Discard 5m → 2233m, same ukeire via different decomposition. Both 7-ukeire tenpai.
Three melds + 55z pair + 12s penchan + 1z floater. The 12s wants 3s, the pair is locked, the East is dead weight. Discard 1z, tenpai on 3s.
